name: Earth
description: Our pale blue dot — a rocky terrestrial planet with a dynamic atmosphere and liquid water oceans, orbiting the Sun at 1 AU.

encyclopedia.overview: Earth, our home planet, is the only known celestial body in the universe to support life. Orbiting the Sun at an average distance of 1 AU, it features a dynamic atmosphere composed of nitrogen and oxygen, active plate tectonics, and vast liquid water oceans covering 71% of its surface. Its magnetic field shields the planet from harmful solar radiation, creating a habitable haven in the cold void of space.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: Earth is in hydrostatic equilibrium, where inward gravity is balanced by outward pressure. It occupies a stable Keplerian orbit around the Sun. Relativistic frame-dragging (the Lense-Thirring effect) caused by its rotation warps local spacetime, which was measured by Gravity Probe B.
interestingFacts: Earth is not a perfect sphere but an oblate spheroid, bulging at the equator due to centrifugal force from its rotation.
The planet's magnetic field is generated by a geodynamo in its liquid iron outer core.
Spacetime is slightly dragged around the Earth as it rotates.
Liquid water exists on its surface thanks to the atmospheric greenhouse effect.

name: Jupiter
description: The solar system's gas giant king — a swirling tempest of hydrogen and helium with the Great Red Spot, a storm raging for centuries.

encyclopedia.overview: Jupiter is the largest planet in our solar system, a massive gas giant containing more than twice the mass of all other planets combined. Made mostly of hydrogen and helium, it lacks a well-defined solid surface. Its atmosphere is marked by colorful, swirling cloud bands driven by powerful jet streams, and is dominated by the Great Red Spot—a massive anticyclonic storm larger than Earth that has raged for centuries.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: Jupiter is in hydrostatic equilibrium, with internal pressures so extreme they compress hydrogen into a metallic liquid state. This metallic hydrogen layer conducts electricity, generating the solar system's strongest planetary magnetic field.
interestingFacts: Jupiter has a magnetic field that is 14 times stronger than Earth's.
The Great Red Spot is a colossal storm that has been observed for over 300 years.
Jupiter acts as a gravitational shield for the inner solar system.
Jupiter radiates more heat into space than it receives from the Sun.

name: Saturn
description: The ringed jewel of the solar system. Its spectacular ring system spans 282,000 km but is only 10–100 meters thick.

encyclopedia.overview: Saturn, the sixth planet from the Sun, is a gas giant second in size only to Jupiter. It is celebrated for its spectacular, highly reflective ring system. The rings are composed of billions of individual particles, ranging from microscopic dust grains to house-sized boulders, which are 99% pure water ice. Saturn has the lowest density of all planets in the solar system—less than that of water.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: Its extreme rotation speed causes significant flattening (oblateness) at the poles. The gravitational tidal forces inside Saturn's Roche limit prevent the icy ring material from accreting into a solid moon.
interestingFacts: Saturn's density is so low that the planet would float in water.
Its magnificent rings are extremely thin, averaging only 10 meters in thickness.
The planet hosts a bizarre, permanent hexagonal storm at its north pole.
Its moon Titan is the only moon in the solar system with a dense atmosphere.

name: PSO J318.5-22
description: A rogue planet drifting through interstellar space without a host star. Its deep crimson atmosphere glows faintly from internal heat.

encyclopedia.overview: PSO J318.5-22 is a celestial body situated in a stable orbital system. It represents a fascinating subject of planetary science, illustrating how gravitational stability and stellar radiation interact to shape the physical properties of orbiting bodies over billions of years.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: Gravitational interaction with its host star forms the primary dynamical force. The system serves as a showcase of planetary physics, illustrating the balance of orbital velocity and gravitational pull.
interestingFacts: Atmospheric temperature profiles on PSO J318.5-22 are highly sensitive to its distance from the central star, exhibiting large changes if the orbit is eccentric.
The orbit of PSO J318.5-22 is governed by Keplerian mechanics, maintaining a stable elliptical path that prevents it from falling into its host star.
Astrometric measurements of PSO J318.5-22 allow researchers to refine models of planetary system evolution and stability in local space.
Gravitational tidal forces from the host star can cause interior heating in PSO J318.5-22, driving volcanic activity or tectonic stress.

name: Proxima Centauri b
description: The nearest known exoplanet, orbiting Proxima Centauri in just 11.2 days within the habitable zone — potentially harboring liquid water.

encyclopedia.overview: Proxima Centauri b is a prominent exoplanetary object orbiting its host star. Observations indicate that its atmospheric dynamics, temperature gradients, and potential magnetosphere are heavily influenced by its orbital proximity and the host star's energy output.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: Gravitational interaction with its host star forms the primary dynamical force. The system serves as a showcase of planetary physics, illustrating the balance of orbital velocity and gravitational pull.
interestingFacts: Astrometric measurements of Proxima Centauri b allow researchers to refine models of planetary system evolution and stability in local space.
Gravitational tidal forces from the host star can cause interior heating in Proxima Centauri b, driving volcanic activity or tectonic stress.
Atmospheric temperature profiles on Proxima Centauri b are highly sensitive to its distance from the central star, exhibiting large changes if the orbit is eccentric.
The orbit of Proxima Centauri b is governed by Keplerian mechanics, maintaining a stable elliptical path that prevents it from falling into its host star.

name: Alpha Centauri AB
description: A binary star system just 4.37 light-years away — the nearest star system to the Sun. Two Sun-like stars locked in gravitational waltz.

encyclopedia.overview: Alpha Centauri AB is a binary stellar system containing two stars locked in a gravitational dance around a shared barycenter. These systems are essential laboratories for stellar astrophysics, as their orbital motions allow for precise measurements of stellar masses and radii.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: The two stars orbit their common center of mass (barycenter). As their separation varies, gravitational tidal forces stretch the stellar envelopes, leading to Roche-lobe deformation.
interestingFacts: In close binary systems like Alpha Centauri AB, gas can stream from the larger star to the smaller companion, forming a hot accretion bridge.
Tidal interactions can cause the stars to bulge toward each other, stretching their spherical shapes into elongated ellipsoids.
The stars in Alpha Centauri AB orbit a common center of mass called the barycenter, which often lies in the empty space between them.

name: Sirius A & B
description: The brightest star in Earth's night sky. Sirius A dominates while Sirius B — a white dwarf — orbits in an elliptical 50-year orbit.

encyclopedia.overview: Sirius A & B consists of two closely bound stellar components. The system shows significant gravitational coupling, where orbital interactions and radiation pressure from both stars shape the local space environment.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: Stellar evolution in Sirius A & B is shaped by the mass transfer between the binary components. Gravitational waves slowly drain orbital energy, causing the orbits to decay over cosmic timescales.
interestingFacts: Mutual eclipses in Sirius A & B provide astronomers with precise data on the stellar diameters, orbital inclinations, and temperatures.
In close binary systems like Sirius A & B, gas can stream from the larger star to the smaller companion, forming a hot accretion bridge.
Tidal interactions can cause the stars to bulge toward each other, stretching their spherical shapes into elongated ellipsoids.
The stars in Sirius A & B orbit a common center of mass called the barycenter, which often lies in the empty space between them.

name: Eta Carinae
description: A hypernova-candidate binary system 7,500 light-years away. The primary is one of the most luminous stars known — a cosmic time bomb.

encyclopedia.overview: Eta Carinae consists of two closely bound stellar components. The system shows significant gravitational coupling, where orbital interactions and radiation pressure from both stars shape the local space environment.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: The two stars orbit their common center of mass (barycenter). As their separation varies, gravitational tidal forces stretch the stellar envelopes, leading to Roche-lobe deformation.
interestingFacts: In close binary systems like Eta Carinae, gas can stream from the larger star to the smaller companion, forming a hot accretion bridge.
Tidal interactions can cause the stars to bulge toward each other, stretching their spherical shapes into elongated ellipsoids.
The stars in Eta Carinae orbit a common center of mass called the barycenter, which often lies in the empty space between them.

name: Sagittarius A*
description: The supermassive black hole at the center of the Milky Way galaxy. Its mass equals 4.1 million suns, warping spacetime dramatically.

encyclopedia.overview: Sagittarius A* (Sgr A*) is the supermassive black hole situated at the dynamical center of the Milky Way galaxy. Packing approximately 4 million solar masses into a region smaller than the orbit of Mercury, it exerts a dominant gravitational influence on surrounding stellar orbits. It is surrounded by a faint, hot accretion flow and serves as a natural laboratory for testing General Relativity in strong gravitational fields.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: It is characterized by an event horizon with a Schwarzschild radius of roughly 12 million kilometers. Gravitational lensing dramatically warps light from the background, creating the characteristic photon ring imaged by the Event Horizon Telescope.
interestingFacts: Sgr A* was directly imaged by the Event Horizon Telescope in 2022.
Stars orbit Sgr A* at speeds exceeding several thousand kilometers per second.
It is currently in an extremely quiet, quiescent state compared to other active galaxies.

name: M87* Black Hole
description: The first black hole ever imaged directly. Located 55 million light-years away in the Virgo galaxy cluster — 6.5 billion solar masses.

encyclopedia.overview: M87* Black Hole is a high-mass gravitational singularity. Observations of the surrounding stellar orbits and accretion flows reveal details about its mass, spin, and the extreme physics occurring just outside its event horizon.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: Matter infalling toward the event horizon is compressed and heated to millions of degrees, emitting energetic X-rays. Magnetic fields channel part of this gas into narrow, relativistic jets.
interestingFacts: Active magnetic fields near the event horizon can launch jets of particles at 99% of the speed of light along the spin axis.
Relativistic Doppler beaming causes the side of the disk rotating toward us to appear significantly brighter than the side moving away.
Gravitational lensing around M87* Black Hole warps the light from the background disk, making the back of the disk appear bent over the top of the black hole.
The boundary of M87* Black Hole is the event horizon, the point of no return where the escape velocity exceeds the speed of light.

name: Cygnus X-1
description: The first black hole ever confirmed through X-ray observation. It voraciously strips matter from its blue supergiant companion HDE 226868.

encyclopedia.overview: Cygnus X-1 is a black hole, an region of spacetime exhibiting gravitational acceleration so strong that nothing—not even light—can escape. It is surrounded by a superheated accretion disk of plasma orbiting at relativistic speeds, radiating intensely across the electromagnetic spectrum.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: Matter infalling toward the event horizon is compressed and heated to millions of degrees, emitting energetic X-rays. Magnetic fields channel part of this gas into narrow, relativistic jets.
interestingFacts: Active magnetic fields near the event horizon can launch jets of particles at 99% of the speed of light along the spin axis.
Relativistic Doppler beaming causes the side of the disk rotating toward us to appear significantly brighter than the side moving away.
Gravitational lensing around Cygnus X-1 warps the light from the background disk, making the back of the disk appear bent over the top of the black hole.
The boundary of Cygnus X-1 is the event horizon, the point of no return where the escape velocity exceeds the speed of light.

name: TON 618
description: One of the most massive black holes ever discovered — 66 billion solar masses, with an event horizon larger than our entire solar system.

encyclopedia.overview: As a stellar compact object, TON 618 represents a boundary where general relativity dominates. The black hole acts as an extreme gravitational sink, drawing in surrounding interstellar gas and dust, forming a glowing disk and relativistic outflows.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: The system is dominated by the event horizon and photon sphere. Light paths are severely bent by gravitational lensing, creating a bright lensed ring of light around a dark central shadow.
interestingFacts: The boundary of TON 618 is the event horizon, the point of no return where the escape velocity exceeds the speed of light.
Active magnetic fields near the event horizon can launch jets of particles at 99% of the speed of light along the spin axis.
Relativistic Doppler beaming causes the side of the disk rotating toward us to appear significantly brighter than the side moving away.

name: Crab Pulsar
description: Born from the supernova of 1054 AD, the Crab Pulsar spins 30 times per second, sweeping X-ray beams across the cosmos.

encyclopedia.overview: The Crab Pulsar (PSR B0531+21) is a young, rapidly rotating neutron star residing at the center of the Crab Nebula (Messier 1). It is the remnant of the historic SN 1054 supernova, which was observed by astronomers worldwide in 1054 AD. Spinning roughly 30 times per second, it emits highly collimated beams of electromagnetic radiation across the spectrum from radio waves to gamma rays.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: It is a rotation-powered pulsar, where the spin-down energy is converted into a relativistic wind of electrons and positrons, forming a Pulsar Wind Nebula. The magnetic field strength reaches a colossal 10^12 Gauss.
interestingFacts: The Crab Pulsar is spinning at 30 rotations per second.
It is slowly losing rotational energy, causing its spin to decelerate.
The star contains more mass than the Sun compressed into a sphere just 20 kilometers wide.

name: Vela Pulsar
description: One of the brightest gamma-ray sources in the sky. Vela spins 11 times per second and is embedded in a spectacular supernova remnant.

encyclopedia.overview: Representing the extreme endpoint of stellar evolution, Vela Pulsar is a pulsar with a magnetosphere of unimaginable intensity. Its fast rotation and powerful magnetic field generate strong electric currents, accelerating particles to relativistic speeds.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: The pulsar's rotation axis is offset from its magnetic dipole axis, causing its emission beams to sweep through space. Relativistic particles flow along the open magnetic field lines, emitting synchrotron radiation.
interestingFacts: Pulsars like Vela Pulsar are composed almost entirely of neutrons, packing the mass of a star into a city-sized sphere.
Sudden increases in the spin rate, known as 'glitches', occur when the pulsar's superfluid core transfers momentum to its crust.
The rotation of Vela Pulsar is so precise that some pulsars can keep time better than the most advanced atomic clocks on Earth.

name: PSR B1919+21
description: The first pulsar ever discovered (1967). Its eerily regular 1.337-second pulses were briefly mistaken for alien communication — dubbed LGM-1.

encyclopedia.overview: PSR B1919+21 is a pulsar—a highly magnetized, rapidly rotating neutron star formed from the core collapse of a massive progenitor star. It emits beams of radiation from its magnetic poles that sweep through space like a lighthouse, producing rhythmic pulses detected by observers.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: The pulsar's rotation axis is offset from its magnetic dipole axis, causing its emission beams to sweep through space. Relativistic particles flow along the open magnetic field lines, emitting synchrotron radiation.
interestingFacts: Pulsars like PSR B1919+21 are composed almost entirely of neutrons, packing the mass of a star into a city-sized sphere.
Sudden increases in the spin rate, known as 'glitches', occur when the pulsar's superfluid core transfers momentum to its crust.
The rotation of PSR B1919+21 is so precise that some pulsars can keep time better than the most advanced atomic clocks on Earth.

name: SGR 1806-20 Magnetar
description: The most magnetic object ever discovered. Released more energy in 0.2s than our Sun does in 250,000 years — a true cosmic cataclysm.

encyclopedia.overview: SGR 1806-20 Magnetar is a pulsar—a highly magnetized, rapidly rotating neutron star formed from the core collapse of a massive progenitor star. It emits beams of radiation from its magnetic poles that sweep through space like a lighthouse, producing rhythmic pulses detected by observers.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: The pulsar's rotation axis is offset from its magnetic dipole axis, causing its emission beams to sweep through space. Relativistic particles flow along the open magnetic field lines, emitting synchrotron radiation.
interestingFacts: Pulsars like SGR 1806-20 Magnetar are composed almost entirely of neutrons, packing the mass of a star into a city-sized sphere.
Sudden increases in the spin rate, known as 'glitches', occur when the pulsar's superfluid core transfers momentum to its crust.
The rotation of SGR 1806-20 Magnetar is so precise that some pulsars can keep time better than the most advanced atomic clocks on Earth.

name: Omega Centauri
description: The largest and most massive globular cluster orbiting the Milky Way — containing ~10 million stars packed into a sphere 150 light-years across.

encyclopedia.overview: Omega Centauri (NGC 5139) is the largest, brightest, and most massive globular cluster orbiting the Milky Way. Located 15,800 light-years away, it contains approximately 10 million stars packed tightly in a sphere about 150 light-years in diameter. Unlike other globular clusters, its stars belong to multiple distinct generations, suggesting it has a complex evolutionary history.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: It exhibits complex stellar dynamics governed by gravitational relaxation. The core has a high stellar density, and astronomers hypothesize it houses an intermediate-mass black hole (IMBH) of 40,000 solar masses.
interestingFacts: Omega Centauri is suspected to be the remnant core of a dwarf galaxy.
The average distance between stars in the core is only 0.1 light-years.
It is visible to the naked eye from the southern hemisphere.

name: Messier 13
description: The Great Hercules Cluster — a landmark globular cluster 22,200 light-years away. Carl Sagan's Arecibo Message was aimed at this target.

encyclopedia.overview: Messier 13 is a gravitationally bound group of stars. As a cluster, it offers a look at stellar families born from the same molecular cloud, allowing astronomers to study stellar evolution across stars of identical age but different masses.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: Tidal forces from the host galaxy slowly strip stars from the outer edges of the cluster, creating long stellar streams that trace the cluster's orbit.
interestingFacts: Frequent close encounters in the dense core can lead to collisions, creating massive, rejuvenated stars called 'blue stragglers'.
Star clusters like Messier 13 are held together by mutual gravity, with stars orbiting the cluster's collective center of mass.
globular clusters like Messier 13 are extremely old, containing stars that formed early in the history of the universe.
Over billions of years, clusters slowly dissolve as gravity from the host galaxy pulls stars away from the outer edges.

name: 47 Tucanae
description: One of the densest and most massive globular clusters, second only to Omega Centauri. Its core harbors exotic pulsars and X-ray binaries.

encyclopedia.overview: Representing a dense stellar swarm, 47 Tucanae is a star cluster that showcases complex gravitational dynamics. The stars orbit a collective center of mass, undergoing frequent close passes that can redistribute orbital energy.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: Tidal forces from the host galaxy slowly strip stars from the outer edges of the cluster, creating long stellar streams that trace the cluster's orbit.
interestingFacts: Frequent close encounters in the dense core can lead to collisions, creating massive, rejuvenated stars called 'blue stragglers'.
Star clusters like 47 Tucanae are held together by mutual gravity, with stars orbiting the cluster's collective center of mass.
globular clusters like 47 Tucanae are extremely old, containing stars that formed early in the history of the universe.
Over billions of years, clusters slowly dissolve as gravity from the host galaxy pulls stars away from the outer edges.

name: TRAPPIST-1e
description: One of seven Earth-sized planets around the ultra-cool dwarf TRAPPIST-1. Prime candidate for life — rocky, temperate, and potentially ocean-covered.

encyclopedia.overview: TRAPPIST-1e is a prominent exoplanetary object orbiting its host star. Observations indicate that its atmospheric dynamics, temperature gradients, and potential magnetosphere are heavily influenced by its orbital proximity and the host star's energy output.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: The physical parameters of TRAPPIST-1e dictate its orbital mechanics. Driven by Kepler's laws, its trajectory is a stable ellipse where orbital velocity fluctuates predictably, peaking at periapsis and dropping at apoapsis.
interestingFacts: Gravitational tidal forces from the host star can cause interior heating in TRAPPIST-1e, driving volcanic activity or tectonic stress.
Atmospheric temperature profiles on TRAPPIST-1e are highly sensitive to its distance from the central star, exhibiting large changes if the orbit is eccentric.
The orbit of TRAPPIST-1e is governed by Keplerian mechanics, maintaining a stable elliptical path that prevents it from falling into its host star.

name: Gliese 667Cc
description: A super-Earth in the habitable zone of a red dwarf, part of a triple-star system. Permanently tidally locked, with one hemisphere in perpetual day.

encyclopedia.overview: As a key member of its planetary system, Gliese 667Cc offers crucial insights into planetary formation and orbital migration. Surrounded by the gravitational fields of its host star, it undergoes regular seasonal transitions and orbital precession.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: The physical parameters of Gliese 667Cc dictate its orbital mechanics. Driven by Kepler's laws, its trajectory is a stable ellipse where orbital velocity fluctuates predictably, peaking at periapsis and dropping at apoapsis.
interestingFacts: The orbit of Gliese 667Cc is governed by Keplerian mechanics, maintaining a stable elliptical path that prevents it from falling into its host star.
Astrometric measurements of Gliese 667Cc allow researchers to refine models of planetary system evolution and stability in local space.
Gravitational tidal forces from the host star can cause interior heating in Gliese 667Cc, driving volcanic activity or tectonic stress.

name: Kepler-452b
description: NASA's 'Earth's cousin' — a super-Earth orbiting a Sun-like star in its habitable zone, 1,400 light-years away. The most Earth-like confirmed exoplanet.

encyclopedia.overview: Kepler-452b is a prominent exoplanetary object orbiting its host star. Observations indicate that its atmospheric dynamics, temperature gradients, and potential magnetosphere are heavily influenced by its orbital proximity and the host star's energy output.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: Gravitational interaction with its host star forms the primary dynamical force. The system serves as a showcase of planetary physics, illustrating the balance of orbital velocity and gravitational pull.
interestingFacts: Atmospheric temperature profiles on Kepler-452b are highly sensitive to its distance from the central star, exhibiting large changes if the orbit is eccentric.
The orbit of Kepler-452b is governed by Keplerian mechanics, maintaining a stable elliptical path that prevents it from falling into its host star.
Astrometric measurements of Kepler-452b allow researchers to refine models of planetary system evolution and stability in local space.
Gravitational tidal forces from the host star can cause interior heating in Kepler-452b, driving volcanic activity or tectonic stress.

name: Wolf 359 System
description: One of the closest star systems to Earth at 7.9 ly — a faint red dwarf famous from Star Trek's Battle of Wolf 359.

encyclopedia.overview: As a key member of its planetary system, Wolf 359 System offers crucial insights into planetary formation and orbital migration. Surrounded by the gravitational fields of its host star, it undergoes regular seasonal transitions and orbital precession.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: The physical parameters of Wolf 359 System dictate its orbital mechanics. Driven by Kepler's laws, its trajectory is a stable ellipse where orbital velocity fluctuates predictably, peaking at periapsis and dropping at apoapsis.
interestingFacts: Gravitational tidal forces from the host star can cause interior heating in Wolf 359 System, driving volcanic activity or tectonic stress.
Atmospheric temperature profiles on Wolf 359 System are highly sensitive to its distance from the central star, exhibiting large changes if the orbit is eccentric.
The orbit of Wolf 359 System is governed by Keplerian mechanics, maintaining a stable elliptical path that prevents it from falling into its host star.

name: UY Scuti
description: One of the largest known stars by radius — a red hypergiant so vast that if placed at the Sun's center, its surface would engulf Jupiter's orbit.

encyclopedia.overview: Representing a dynamic pair of stars, UY Scuti illustrates the complex physics of binary systems. Depending on their separation, the stars may exchange matter, undergo mutual eclipses, and experience tidal distortions that alter their shapes.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: Stellar evolution in UY Scuti is shaped by the mass transfer between the binary components. Gravitational waves slowly drain orbital energy, causing the orbits to decay over cosmic timescales.
interestingFacts: Mutual eclipses in UY Scuti provide astronomers with precise data on the stellar diameters, orbital inclinations, and temperatures.
In close binary systems like UY Scuti, gas can stream from the larger star to the smaller companion, forming a hot accretion bridge.
Tidal interactions can cause the stars to bulge toward each other, stretching their spherical shapes into elongated ellipsoids.
The stars in UY Scuti orbit a common center of mass called the barycenter, which often lies in the empty space between them.

name: PSR J0437-4715
description: The brightest and nearest millisecond pulsar — spinning 174 times per second with clockwork precision. A natural cosmic laboratory for extreme physics.

encyclopedia.overview: PSR J0437-4715 is a pulsar—a highly magnetized, rapidly rotating neutron star formed from the core collapse of a massive progenitor star. It emits beams of radiation from its magnetic poles that sweep through space like a lighthouse, producing rhythmic pulses detected by observers.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: The pulsar's rotation axis is offset from its magnetic dipole axis, causing its emission beams to sweep through space. Relativistic particles flow along the open magnetic field lines, emitting synchrotron radiation.
interestingFacts: Pulsars like PSR J0437-4715 are composed almost entirely of neutrons, packing the mass of a star into a city-sized sphere.
Sudden increases in the spin rate, known as 'glitches', occur when the pulsar's superfluid core transfers momentum to its crust.
The rotation of PSR J0437-4715 is so precise that some pulsars can keep time better than the most advanced atomic clocks on Earth.

name: NGC 1300 Nucleus
description: The nuclear star cluster at the center of the barred spiral galaxy NGC 1300, 61 million light-years away — a dense hub of ancient stars.

encyclopedia.overview: Representing a dense stellar swarm, NGC 1300 Nucleus is a star cluster that showcases complex gravitational dynamics. The stars orbit a collective center of mass, undergoing frequent close passes that can redistribute orbital energy.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: Tidal forces from the host galaxy slowly strip stars from the outer edges of the cluster, creating long stellar streams that trace the cluster's orbit.
interestingFacts: Frequent close encounters in the dense core can lead to collisions, creating massive, rejuvenated stars called 'blue stragglers'.
Star clusters like NGC 1300 Nucleus are held together by mutual gravity, with stars orbiting the cluster's collective center of mass.
globular clusters like NGC 1300 Nucleus are extremely old, containing stars that formed early in the history of the universe.
Over billions of years, clusters slowly dissolve as gravity from the host galaxy pulls stars away from the outer edges.

name: 1I/'Oumuamua
description: The first confirmed interstellar object to pass through our Solar System, executing a high-speed hyperbolic flyby.

encyclopedia.overview: As a key member of its planetary system, 1I/'Oumuamua offers crucial insights into planetary formation and orbital migration. Surrounded by the gravitational fields of its host star, it undergoes regular seasonal transitions and orbital precession.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: The physical parameters of 1I/'Oumuamua dictate its orbital mechanics. Driven by Kepler's laws, its trajectory is a stable ellipse where orbital velocity fluctuates predictably, peaking at periapsis and dropping at apoapsis.
interestingFacts: Gravitational tidal forces from the host star can cause interior heating in 1I/'Oumuamua, driving volcanic activity or tectonic stress.
Atmospheric temperature profiles on 1I/'Oumuamua are highly sensitive to its distance from the central star, exhibiting large changes if the orbit is eccentric.
The orbit of 1I/'Oumuamua is governed by Keplerian mechanics, maintaining a stable elliptical path that prevents it from falling into its host star.

name: Algol (Demon Star)
description: The prototype eclipsing binary star. Teaches the 'Algol Paradox' where a less massive star has evolved faster due to stellar mass transfer.

encyclopedia.overview: Algol (Demon Star) consists of two closely bound stellar components. The system shows significant gravitational coupling, where orbital interactions and radiation pressure from both stars shape the local space environment.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: Stellar evolution in Algol (Demon Star) is shaped by the mass transfer between the binary components. Gravitational waves slowly drain orbital energy, causing the orbits to decay over cosmic timescales.
interestingFacts: Mutual eclipses in Algol (Demon Star) provide astronomers with precise data on the stellar diameters, orbital inclinations, and temperatures.
In close binary systems like Algol (Demon Star), gas can stream from the larger star to the smaller companion, forming a hot accretion bridge.
Tidal interactions can cause the stars to bulge toward each other, stretching their spherical shapes into elongated ellipsoids.
The stars in Algol (Demon Star) orbit a common center of mass called the barycenter, which often lies in the empty space between them.

name: PSR B1257+12 (Lich)
description: The first system of exoplanets ever discovered (1992), orbiting a rapidly spinning pulsar. These 'zombie planets' survive in an extreme radiation field.

encyclopedia.overview: PSR B1257+12 (Lich) is a dense, spinning stellar remnant. It exhibits intense physical conditions, combining nuclear-density matter, extreme magnetic forces, and millisecond-scale rotations that test the limits of modern physics.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: The pulsar's rotation axis is offset from its magnetic dipole axis, causing its emission beams to sweep through space. Relativistic particles flow along the open magnetic field lines, emitting synchrotron radiation.
interestingFacts: Pulsars like PSR B1257+12 (Lich) are composed almost entirely of neutrons, packing the mass of a star into a city-sized sphere.
Sudden increases in the spin rate, known as 'glitches', occur when the pulsar's superfluid core transfers momentum to its crust.
The rotation of PSR B1257+12 (Lich) is so precise that some pulsars can keep time better than the most advanced atomic clocks on Earth.

name: Pleiades (Seven Sisters)
description: The Seven Sisters - a nearby open cluster dominated by hot, luminous blue stars, embedded in a beautiful reflection nebula.

encyclopedia.overview: Pleiades (Seven Sisters) is a gravitationally bound group of stars. As a cluster, it offers a look at stellar families born from the same molecular cloud, allowing astronomers to study stellar evolution across stars of identical age but different masses.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: Tidal forces from the host galaxy slowly strip stars from the outer edges of the cluster, creating long stellar streams that trace the cluster's orbit.
interestingFacts: globular clusters like Pleiades (Seven Sisters) are extremely old, containing stars that formed early in the history of the universe.
Over billions of years, clusters slowly dissolve as gravity from the host galaxy pulls stars away from the outer edges.
Frequent close encounters in the dense core can lead to collisions, creating massive, rejuvenated stars called 'blue stragglers'.
Star clusters like Pleiades (Seven Sisters) are held together by mutual gravity, with stars orbiting the cluster's collective center of mass.

name: KIC 8462852 (Tabby's Star)
description: The star with erratic, non-periodic dips in brightness. Teaches the physics of stellar occultation and dust-light scattering.

encyclopedia.overview: As a key member of its planetary system, KIC 8462852 (Tabby's Star) offers crucial insights into planetary formation and orbital migration. Surrounded by the gravitational fields of its host star, it undergoes regular seasonal transitions and orbital precession.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: The physical parameters of KIC 8462852 (Tabby's Star) dictate its orbital mechanics. Driven by Kepler's laws, its trajectory is a stable ellipse where orbital velocity fluctuates predictably, peaking at periapsis and dropping at apoapsis.
interestingFacts: The orbit of KIC 8462852 (Tabby's Star) is governed by Keplerian mechanics, maintaining a stable elliptical path that prevents it from falling into its host star.
Astrometric measurements of KIC 8462852 (Tabby's Star) allow researchers to refine models of planetary system evolution and stability in local space.
Gravitational tidal forces from the host star can cause interior heating in KIC 8462852 (Tabby's Star), driving volcanic activity or tectonic stress.

name: 55 Cancri e (Janssen)
description: A carbon-rich super-Earth orbiting so closely that its surface is a permanent sea of lava, and its interior may be rich in diamond.

encyclopedia.overview: 55 Cancri e (Janssen) is a celestial body situated in a stable orbital system. It represents a fascinating subject of planetary science, illustrating how gravitational stability and stellar radiation interact to shape the physical properties of orbiting bodies over billions of years.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: Gravitational interaction with its host star forms the primary dynamical force. The system serves as a showcase of planetary physics, illustrating the balance of orbital velocity and gravitational pull.
interestingFacts: Atmospheric temperature profiles on 55 Cancri e (Janssen) are highly sensitive to its distance from the central star, exhibiting large changes if the orbit is eccentric.
The orbit of 55 Cancri e (Janssen) is governed by Keplerian mechanics, maintaining a stable elliptical path that prevents it from falling into its host star.
Astrometric measurements of 55 Cancri e (Janssen) allow researchers to refine models of planetary system evolution and stability in local space.
Gravitational tidal forces from the host star can cause interior heating in 55 Cancri e (Janssen), driving volcanic activity or tectonic stress.

name: HD 189733b
description: A cobalt-blue hot Jupiter where supersonic winds drive sideways rain composed of vaporized liquid glass.

encyclopedia.overview: HD 189733b is a celestial body situated in a stable orbital system. It represents a fascinating subject of planetary science, illustrating how gravitational stability and stellar radiation interact to shape the physical properties of orbiting bodies over billions of years.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: The physical parameters of HD 189733b dictate its orbital mechanics. Driven by Kepler's laws, its trajectory is a stable ellipse where orbital velocity fluctuates predictably, peaking at periapsis and dropping at apoapsis.
interestingFacts: Gravitational tidal forces from the host star can cause interior heating in HD 189733b, driving volcanic activity or tectonic stress.
Atmospheric temperature profiles on HD 189733b are highly sensitive to its distance from the central star, exhibiting large changes if the orbit is eccentric.
The orbit of HD 189733b is governed by Keplerian mechanics, maintaining a stable elliptical path that prevents it from falling into its host star.

name: Gliese 581g (Zarmina)
description: A disputed super-Earth exoplanet claimed to orbit in the middle of a red dwarf's habitable zone.

encyclopedia.overview: Gliese 581g (Zarmina) is a celestial body situated in a stable orbital system. It represents a fascinating subject of planetary science, illustrating how gravitational stability and stellar radiation interact to shape the physical properties of orbiting bodies over billions of years.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: Gravitational interaction with its host star forms the primary dynamical force. The system serves as a showcase of planetary physics, illustrating the balance of orbital velocity and gravitational pull.
interestingFacts: Astrometric measurements of Gliese 581g (Zarmina) allow researchers to refine models of planetary system evolution and stability in local space.
Gravitational tidal forces from the host star can cause interior heating in Gliese 581g (Zarmina), driving volcanic activity or tectonic stress.

The orbit of Gliese 581g (Zarmina) is governed by Keplerian mechanics, maintaining a stable elliptical path that prevents it from falling into its host star.

name: Castor System
description: A complex hierarchical sextuple star system containing six stars orbiting in three distinct binary pairs.

encyclopedia.overview: Representing a dynamic pair of stars, Castor System illustrates the complex physics of binary systems. Depending on their separation, the stars may exchange matter, undergo mutual eclipses, and experience tidal distortions that alter their shapes.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: The two stars orbit their common center of mass (barycenter). As their separation varies, gravitational tidal forces stretch the stellar envelopes, leading to Roche-lobe deformation.
interestingFacts: The stars in Castor System orbit a common center of mass called the barycenter, which often lies in the empty space between them.
Mutual eclipses in Castor System provide astronomers with precise data on the stellar diameters, orbital inclinations, and temperatures.
In close binary systems like Castor System, gas can stream from the larger star to the smaller companion, forming a hot accretion bridge.

classificationData.Companion Stars: M-type Red Dwarfs (Castor Ab, Bb, YY Gem AB)

name: Kepler-16 System
description: The first confirmed circumbinary planet system, where a planet orbits two stars in a Tatooine-like configuration.

encyclopedia.overview: Kepler-16 System consists of two closely bound stellar components. The system shows significant gravitational coupling, where orbital interactions and radiation pressure from both stars shape the local space environment.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: Stellar evolution in Kepler-16 System is shaped by the mass transfer between the binary components. Gravitational waves slowly drain orbital energy, causing the orbits to decay over cosmic timescales.
interestingFacts: Mutual eclipses in Kepler-16 System provide astronomers with precise data on the stellar diameters, orbital inclinations, and temperatures.
In close binary systems like Kepler-16 System, gas can stream from the larger star to the smaller companion, forming a hot accretion bridge.
Tidal interactions can cause the stars to bulge toward each other, stretching their spherical shapes into elongated ellipsoids.
The stars in Kepler-16 System orbit a common center of mass called the barycenter, which often lies in the empty space between them.

name: V616 Monocerotis
description: One of the closest known stellar-mass black holes to Earth. It pulls gas from its orange dwarf companion in a 7.7-hour orbit.

encyclopedia.overview: V616 Monocerotis is a high-mass gravitational singularity. Observations of the surrounding stellar orbits and accretion flows reveal details about its mass, spin, and the extreme physics occurring just outside its event horizon.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: The system is dominated by the event horizon and photon sphere. Light paths are severely bent by gravitational lensing, creating a bright lensed ring of light around a dark central shadow.
interestingFacts: Relativistic Doppler beaming causes the side of the disk rotating toward us to appear significantly brighter than the side moving away.
Gravitational lensing around V616 Monocerotis warps the light from the background disk, making the back of the disk appear bent over the top of the black hole.
The boundary of V616 Monocerotis is the event horizon, the point of no return where the escape velocity exceeds the speed of light.

name: GRO J1655-40
description: A galactic microquasar famous for ejections at apparent superluminal speeds (1.1c) due to geometric projection effects.

encyclopedia.overview: As a stellar compact object, GRO J1655-40 represents a boundary where general relativity dominates. The black hole acts as an extreme gravitational sink, drawing in surrounding interstellar gas and dust, forming a glowing disk and relativistic outflows.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: The system is dominated by the event horizon and photon sphere. Light paths are severely bent by gravitational lensing, creating a bright lensed ring of light around a dark central shadow.
interestingFacts: The boundary of GRO J1655-40 is the event horizon, the point of no return where the escape velocity exceeds the speed of light.
Active magnetic fields near the event horizon can launch jets of particles at 99% of the speed of light along the spin axis.
Relativistic Doppler beaming causes the side of the disk rotating toward us to appear significantly brighter than the side moving away.

name: Hulse-Taylor Pulsar
description: The first binary pulsar. Its orbital decay confirmed General Relativity's prediction of gravitational waves.

encyclopedia.overview: Hulse-Taylor Pulsar is a pulsar—a highly magnetized, rapidly rotating neutron star formed from the core collapse of a massive progenitor star. It emits beams of radiation from its magnetic poles that sweep through space like a lighthouse, producing rhythmic pulses detected by observers.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: Extreme density inside Hulse-Taylor Pulsar compresses atomic nuclei into a neutron degenerate fluid. The conservation of angular momentum during core collapse is what drives its incredibly fast spin rate.
interestingFacts: Its magnetic field is trillions of times stronger than Earth's, powerful enough to distort the atomic shapes of elements.
Pulsars like Hulse-Taylor Pulsar are composed almost entirely of neutrons, packing the mass of a star into a city-sized sphere.
Sudden increases in the spin rate, known as 'glitches', occur when the pulsar's superfluid core transfers momentum to its crust.
The rotation of Hulse-Taylor Pulsar is so precise that some pulsars can keep time better than the most advanced atomic clocks on Earth.

name: Diamond Planet Pulsar
description: PSR J1719-1438 is orbited by a companion star stripped down to its carbon core, crystallized into solid diamond.

encyclopedia.overview: Diamond Planet Pulsar is a dense, spinning stellar remnant. It exhibits intense physical conditions, combining nuclear-density matter, extreme magnetic forces, and millisecond-scale rotations that test the limits of modern physics.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: Extreme density inside Diamond Planet Pulsar compresses atomic nuclei into a neutron degenerate fluid. The conservation of angular momentum during core collapse is what drives its incredibly fast spin rate.
interestingFacts: Its magnetic field is trillions of times stronger than Earth's, powerful enough to distort the atomic shapes of elements.
Pulsars like Diamond Planet Pulsar are composed almost entirely of neutrons, packing the mass of a star into a city-sized sphere.
Sudden increases in the spin rate, known as 'glitches', occur when the pulsar's superfluid core transfers momentum to its crust.
The rotation of Diamond Planet Pulsar is so precise that some pulsars can keep time better than the most advanced atomic clocks on Earth.

name: R136 Super Star Cluster
description: A dense starburst cluster in the Large Magellanic Cloud hosting R136a1, the most massive star known (~250 M☉).

encyclopedia.overview: Representing a dense stellar swarm, R136 Super Star Cluster is a star cluster that showcases complex gravitational dynamics. The stars orbit a collective center of mass, undergoing frequent close passes that can redistribute orbital energy.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: The gravitational interactions in R136 Super Star Cluster follow the Plummer potential model, showing a dense core that gradually thins out at the edges. Mass segregation drives massive stars toward the core, while lighter stars drift outward.
interestingFacts: Star clusters like R136 Super Star Cluster are held together by mutual gravity, with stars orbiting the cluster's collective center of mass.
globular clusters like R136 Super Star Cluster are extremely old, containing stars that formed early in the history of the universe.
Over billions of years, clusters slowly dissolve as gravity from the host galaxy pulls stars away from the outer edges.

name: Hyades Open Cluster
description: The closest open cluster to Earth. Its stars are loosely bound by gravity and form the V-shape of Taurus.

encyclopedia.overview: Hyades Open Cluster is a gravitationally bound group of stars. As a cluster, it offers a look at stellar families born from the same molecular cloud, allowing astronomers to study stellar evolution across stars of identical age but different masses.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: The gravitational interactions in Hyades Open Cluster follow the Plummer potential model, showing a dense core that gradually thins out at the edges. Mass segregation drives massive stars toward the core, while lighter stars drift outward.
interestingFacts: Over billions of years, clusters slowly dissolve as gravity from the host galaxy pulls stars away from the outer edges.
Frequent close encounters in the dense core can lead to collisions, creating massive, rejuvenated stars called 'blue stragglers'.
Star clusters like Hyades Open Cluster are held together by mutual gravity, with stars orbiting the cluster's collective center of mass.

name: CoRoT-7b
description: A scorched rocky super-Earth where silicate temperatures are so high that it literally rains rocks from a vaporized stone atmosphere.

encyclopedia.overview: CoRoT-7b is a prominent exoplanetary object orbiting its host star. Observations indicate that its atmospheric dynamics, temperature gradients, and potential magnetosphere are heavily influenced by its orbital proximity and the host star's energy output.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: Gravitational interaction with its host star forms the primary dynamical force. The system serves as a showcase of planetary physics, illustrating the balance of orbital velocity and gravitational pull.
interestingFacts: Atmospheric temperature profiles on CoRoT-7b are highly sensitive to its distance from the central star, exhibiting large changes if the orbit is eccentric.
The orbit of CoRoT-7b is governed by Keplerian mechanics, maintaining a stable elliptical path that prevents it from falling into its host star.
Astrometric measurements of CoRoT-7b allow researchers to refine models of planetary system evolution and stability in local space.
Gravitational tidal forces from the host star can cause interior heating in CoRoT-7b, driving volcanic activity or tectonic stress.

name: KELT-9b
description: The hottest known exoplanet, with a daytime temperature of 4,300°C — hotter than most stars — causing molecules to rip apart into atomic form.

name: Spica
description: A close binary star system where two massive blue giants are distorted into rotating egg-shapes by extreme tidal forces.

encyclopedia.overview: Spica is a binary stellar system containing two stars locked in a gravitational dance around a shared barycenter. These systems are essential laboratories for stellar astrophysics, as their orbital motions allow for precise measurements of stellar masses and radii.

interestingFacts: The stars in Spica orbit a common center of mass called the barycenter, which often lies in the empty space between them.

name: PSR J0737-3039 (Double Pulsar)
description: The only known double pulsar system, where two neutron stars orbit each other in just 2.4 hours, creating a massive gravitational wave emission.

encyclopedia.overview: PSR J0737-3039 (Double Pulsar) consists of two closely bound stellar components. The system shows significant gravitational coupling, where orbital interactions and radiation pressure from both stars shape the local space environment.

name: H1743-322
description: An active stellar-mass black hole candidate famous for producing periodic 'bullet-like' radio jet ejections.

encyclopedia.overview: H1743-322 is a high-mass gravitational singularity. Observations of the surrounding stellar orbits and accretion flows reveal details about its mass, spin, and the extreme physics occurring just outside its event horizon.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: Matter infalling toward the event horizon is compressed and heated to millions of degrees, emitting energetic X-rays. Magnetic fields channel part of this gas into narrow, relativistic jets.
interestingFacts: Active magnetic fields near the event horizon can launch jets of particles at 99% of the speed of light along the spin axis.
Relativistic Doppler beaming causes the side of the disk rotating toward us to appear significantly brighter than the side moving away.
Gravitational lensing around H1743-322 warps the light from the background disk, making the back of the disk appear bent over the top of the black hole.
The boundary of H1743-322 is the event horizon, the point of no return where the escape velocity exceeds the speed of light.

name: CO-0.40-0.22* (IMBH)
description: A candidate intermediate-mass black hole (~100,000 M☉) lurking in a molecular gas cloud near the center of the Milky Way.

encyclopedia.overview: CO-0.40-0.22* (IMBH) is a high-mass gravitational singularity. Observations of the surrounding stellar orbits and accretion flows reveal details about its mass, spin, and the extreme physics occurring just outside its event horizon.

name: Geminga
description: A quiet pulsar that is one of the brightest gamma-ray sources in the night sky, yet is completely silent in radio emissions.

encyclopedia.overview: Geminga is a dense, spinning stellar remnant. It exhibits intense physical conditions, combining nuclear-density matter, extreme magnetic forces, and millisecond-scale rotations that test the limits of modern physics.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: The pulsar's rotation axis is offset from its magnetic dipole axis, causing its emission beams to sweep through space. Relativistic particles flow along the open magnetic field lines, emitting synchrotron radiation.
interestingFacts: Pulsars like Geminga are composed almost entirely of neutrons, packing the mass of a star into a city-sized sphere.
Sudden increases in the spin rate, known as 'glitches', occur when the pulsar's superfluid core transfers momentum to its crust.
The rotation of Geminga is so precise that some pulsars can keep time better than the most advanced atomic clocks on Earth.

name: Messier 15
description: An ancient globular cluster that has undergone core collapse, creating an extremely dense central stellar concentration.

encyclopedia.overview: Messier 15 is a star cluster containing thousands to millions of stars bound together by their mutual gravity. These clusters represent some of the oldest structures in the universe, providing key clues about galaxy formation and stellar dynamics.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: The gravitational interactions in Messier 15 follow the Plummer potential model, showing a dense core that gradually thins out at the edges. Mass segregation drives massive stars toward the core, while lighter stars drift outward.
interestingFacts: Over billions of years, clusters slowly dissolve as gravity from the host galaxy pulls stars away from the outer edges.
Frequent close encounters in the dense core can lead to collisions, creating massive, rejuvenated stars called 'blue stragglers'.
Star clusters like Messier 15 are held together by mutual gravity, with stars orbiting the cluster's collective center of mass.

name: Eagle Nebula Cluster (NGC 6611)
description: The young, active open cluster that ionizes and illuminates the towering columns of dust in the Pillars of Creation.

encyclopedia.overview: Eagle Nebula Cluster (NGC 6611) is a gravitationally bound group of stars. As a cluster, it offers a look at stellar families born from the same molecular cloud, allowing astronomers to study stellar evolution across stars of identical age but different masses.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: Tidal forces from the host galaxy slowly strip stars from the outer edges of the cluster, creating long stellar streams that trace the cluster's orbit.
interestingFacts: Frequent close encounters in the dense core can lead to collisions, creating massive, rejuvenated stars called 'blue stragglers'.
Star clusters like Eagle Nebula Cluster (NGC 6611) are held together by mutual gravity, with stars orbiting the cluster's collective center of mass.
globular clusters like Eagle Nebula Cluster (NGC 6611) are extremely old, containing stars that formed early in the history of the universe.
Over billions of years, clusters slowly dissolve as gravity from the host galaxy pulls stars away from the outer edges.

name: Jewel Box Cluster (NGC 4755)
description: A beautiful galactic open cluster in Crux, featuring contrasting blue stars grouped around a central bright red supergiant.

encyclopedia.overview: Jewel Box Cluster (NGC 4755) is a star cluster containing thousands to millions of stars bound together by their mutual gravity. These clusters represent some of the oldest structures in the universe, providing key clues about galaxy formation and stellar dynamics.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: Tidal forces from the host galaxy slowly strip stars from the outer edges of the cluster, creating long stellar streams that trace the cluster's orbit.
interestingFacts: Frequent close encounters in the dense core can lead to collisions, creating massive, rejuvenated stars called 'blue stragglers'.
Star clusters like Jewel Box Cluster (NGC 4755) are held together by mutual gravity, with stars orbiting the cluster's collective center of mass.
globular clusters like Jewel Box Cluster (NGC 4755) are extremely old, containing stars that formed early in the history of the universe.
Over billions of years, clusters slowly dissolve as gravity from the host galaxy pulls stars away from the outer edges.

classificationData.Spectral Class Dominance: B-type blue supergiants, M-type red supergiant

name: Westerlund 1 Super Cluster
description: One of the most massive young super star clusters in our galaxy, rich in yellow, red, and blue hypergiants.

encyclopedia.overview: Representing a dense stellar swarm, Westerlund 1 Super Cluster is a star cluster that showcases complex gravitational dynamics. The stars orbit a collective center of mass, undergoing frequent close passes that can redistribute orbital energy.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: Tidal forces from the host galaxy slowly strip stars from the outer edges of the cluster, creating long stellar streams that trace the cluster's orbit.
interestingFacts: globular clusters like Westerlund 1 Super Cluster are extremely old, containing stars that formed early in the history of the universe.
Over billions of years, clusters slowly dissolve as gravity from the host galaxy pulls stars away from the outer edges.
Frequent close encounters in the dense core can lead to collisions, creating massive, rejuvenated stars called 'blue stragglers'.
Star clusters like Westerlund 1 Super Cluster are held together by mutual gravity, with stars orbiting the cluster's collective center of mass.

classificationData.Type: Super Star Cluster / Young Massive Cluster

name: Andromeda-Milky Way Collision
description: A simulated collision between the Milky Way and Andromeda galaxies, occurring 4 billion years in the future.

encyclopedia.overview: The Andromeda-Milky Way Collision is a predicted galactic merger expected to occur in approximately 4.5 billion years between the Milky Way and the Andromeda Galaxy. Currently separated by 2.5 million light-years, the two galaxies are rushing toward each other at 110 kilometers per second, pulled by their mutual gravitational attraction.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: This is a massive N-body gravitational interaction. Dynamic tidal forces will tear spiral arms into long tidal tails, compress interstellar gas clouds to trigger starburst events, and eventually merge both supermassive black holes.
interestingFacts: Stars will almost certainly not collide during the merger.
The merger will transform both spiral galaxies into a giant elliptical galaxy.
Earth's night sky will be dominated by a spectacular glowing cosmic display.

name: Betelgeuse Supernova
description: The spectacular death of a red supergiant star, leading to core collapse and an expanding shockwave.

encyclopedia.overview: The Betelgeuse Supernova is the impending cataclysmic death of Betelgeuse, a red supergiant star marking the shoulder of the Orion constellation. Having exhausted the hydrogen fuel in its core, Betelgeuse is now fusing heavier elements and is highly unstable, undergoing large pulsations in size and brightness. Within the next 100,000 years, it will collapse under its own weight, triggering a spectacular core-collapse supernova.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: When the iron core collapses, it bounces back off the ultra-dense nuclear core, generating a shockwave powered by an enormous burst of neutrinos. This ejects the star's envelope into space at a fraction of the speed of light.
interestingFacts: The supernova will be bright enough to be seen in broad daylight.
It will leave behind either a dense neutron star or a stellar black hole.
At 640 light-years away, the explosion will pose no threat to life on Earth.

name: Caldwell 92 (Carina Nebula)
description: The Carina Nebula is a large, complex area of bright and dark nebulosity in the constellation Carina, housing several massive star clusters including Trumpler 14 and Trumpler 16, as well as the famous hypergiant Eta Carinae.

encyclopedia.overview: Caldwell 92, also known as the Carina Nebula (NGC 3372), is one of the largest diffuse nebulae in our skies. Although it is some four times as large and even brighter than the famous Orion Nebula, the Carina Nebula is much less well known due to its location in the southern sky.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: The nebula is a vast region of star formation containing intense radiation fields from massive O-type stars, which ionize the surrounding hydrogen gas. The resulting stellar winds carve immense hollows into the molecular clouds.
interestingFacts: Contains Eta Carinae, a highly unstable binary star system that is one of the most massive and luminous in the Milky Way.
Home to the Homunculus Nebula, formed during an eruption of Eta Carinae in the 1840s.
Discovered by Nicolas-Louis de Lacaille in 1752 from the Cape of Good Hope.

name: Caldwell 77 (Centaurus A)
description: Centaurus A is a prominent peculiar galaxy in the constellation Centaurus, known for its massive dust lane and extreme radio emissions resulting from a galactic merger and a supermassive black hole.

encyclopedia.overview: Caldwell 77 (NGC 5128), widely known as Centaurus A, is one of the closest radio galaxies to Earth. Its unique appearance—an elliptical galaxy split by a thick, dark band of dust—is the result of a past collision with a smaller spiral galaxy.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: The center of the galaxy hosts a supermassive black hole with a mass of 55 million suns, which ejects relativistic jets responsible for massive radio lobes spanning over a million light-years across space.
interestingFacts: It is the fifth-brightest galaxy in the sky, making it an ideal target for amateur astronomers in the southern hemisphere.
The dark dust lane contains immense amounts of molecular gas and is a site of vigorous star formation triggered by the galactic collision.
Its relativistic jets move at about half the speed of light.

name: Caldwell 69 (Bug Nebula)
description: The Bug Nebula is a striking bipolar planetary nebula in Scorpius. It boasts one of the hottest known central stars, generating exceptionally high-velocity stellar winds and a complex, highly structured dust torus.

encyclopedia.overview: Caldwell 69 (NGC 6302), often called the Bug or Butterfly Nebula, is a bipolar planetary nebula. The structure is one of the most complex ever observed in planetary nebulae, shaped by an incredibly hot central star.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: The central star has an estimated surface temperature of over 250,000 degrees Celsius, radiating intensely in ultraviolet light. It is hidden by an equatorial disk of dust, which pinches the outflowing gas into a bipolar butterfly shape.
interestingFacts: The dust torus contains hydrocarbons, carbonates, and water ice, an unusual mix suggesting complex late-stage stellar chemistry.
The gas in its 'wings' is expanding through space at over 600 kilometers per second.
Despite its heat, the central star has never been directly observed visually because of the dense dust lane.

name: Caldwell 104 (NGC 362)
description: NGC 362 is an extraordinarily dense globular cluster located near the Small Magellanic Cloud in the southern sky. It possesses a highly concentrated core and an unusual age relative to other Milky Way globulars.

encyclopedia.overview: Caldwell 104 is a prominent globular cluster. Although often overshadowed by its brilliant neighbor 47 Tucanae, NGC 362 is a magnificent object in its own right, distinguished by an unusually high concentration of stars in its core.
encyclopedia.physicsHighlights: Studies of its stellar population reveal an unusually high abundance of 'blue stragglers'—stars that appear younger and hotter than their neighbors, likely formed through stellar collisions in the dense core environment.
interestingFacts: NGC 362 is a 'young' globular cluster, estimated to be between 10 and 11 billion years old, significantly younger than the Milky Way's oldest clusters.
It passes extremely close to the center of the Milky Way during its highly eccentric orbit.
It was discovered by James Dunlop in 1826.
